company: Northwind — the 6-person launch team (5 engineers + a product lead) shipping the Northwind v1.0 mobile app

costs._note: Refocus tax: work-minutes the recipient loses per CHAT the PM sends (serialized; email is exempt).

_answer_note: A blocked engineer unblocks instantly on a CHAT reply; an EMAIL reply lands only on the next 120-min grid tick.
_note: Mobile app v1.0 launch, due Friday 17:00. Deep critical path (design -> api -> iOS/Android -> QA) with a heavy API bottleneck that IDLES the mobile engineers until it clears — the central lesson is to swarm/unblock the bottleneck rather than let the team sit. Hidden physics: Sam is fast on backend, Ivy/Arjun on their platforms; the API's '~8h left' is really ~36h (a payments-SDK integration blew up, surfaces Tuesday); the polished marketing site is a P2 herring; the privacy review looks like a 1h formality but is 4h; and Sam is BLOCKED on the auth-flow decision until the PM calls it.

tasks.title: Design system + screen specs (dependency for the app builds)
Backend API (auth, payments, sync) — the app depends on it

questions.ping: I need your call on the auth flow before I can lock the API contract: do we ship the new OAuth/social login for v1, or keep the simple email+password and add OAuth post-launch? The payments and sync endpoints hang off this decision and I'm blocked until you choose. One-line answer, but I can't move the API forward till I hear back.

belief.note: the third-party payments SDK integration is far worse than I thought — undocumented webhooks, retries, reconciliation. Realistically ~two-thirds of the work is still ahead, not the polish I claimed. Friday is at serious risk

tasks.title: Polished marketing landing page + launch video

task_arrivals.announce: App Review's privacy questionnaire just came in — data collection disclosures, third-party SDK list, a data-deletion flow — and it MUST be submitted before we can ship. Tell Alex it exists, that it looks like a quick formality (an hour, you'd guess), and pointedly ask who owns it, because right now nobody does.

task.title: App Store privacy review + data-deletion disclosures (submission blocker)
task.reported: Landed on Sam by default — he is underwater on the API

beats.intent: The Friday launch is on your mind and you want a concrete, current status picture from Alex today. React naturally to however your conversation stands — don't assume anything your chat history contradicts.

It's Thursday morning, launch is tomorrow 17:00, and QA still hasn't started because the app builds aren't done. You need a firm answer about Friday today, before you brief the exec team. React naturally given whatever Alex has or hasn't told you — pointed if you've been kept in the dark, focused on the decision if you've been kept informed.

npcs.name: Sam Rivera
npcs.persona: Strong backend engineer, direct, but guarded about his own slippage. Hedges when asked vaguely about progress, only comes clean about problems when asked a direct, specific question. Not rude — just defensive about the API being behind.
knowledge: You own the backend API (auth, payments, sync) — the whole app depends on it, due Friday 17:00 for launch.
As of Monday you told everyone it's nearly done — you haven't re-checked since you started the payments SDK integration.
The design specs landed 2 days late, which is part of why you're behind, but you don't like blaming teammates.
The iOS and Android builds can't really start until you lock the API contract — three engineers are effectively waiting on you.
You are blocked on one decision only the PM/product can make: whether v1 ships OAuth/social login or simple email+password. You can't lock the API until that's decided.

npcs.name: Ivy Park
npcs.persona: Sharp and proactive, a little impatient. Speaks up when she's blocked. Happy to help elsewhere if the PM asks — she's fast and knows she's currently idle waiting on the API.
knowledge: You own the iOS build. You can't make real progress until Sam locks the API contract, so you're mostly idle right now.
You're strong on iOS and decent on general mobile/app-integration work.
If the PM asks, you'd happily pair with Sam on the API to help unblock everyone, or take on other mobile work.

npcs.name: Arjun Mehta
npcs.persona: Steady and thorough, less talkative. Answers what he's asked. Also idle waiting on the API but won't volunteer that unless asked directly.
knowledge: You own the Android build. Like Ivy, you're blocked until the API contract is locked.
You're strong on Android and decent on general mobile work.
You'd help on the API or elsewhere if the PM explicitly asks, but you won't push for it.

npcs.name: Quinn Alvarez
npcs.persona: Detail-obsessed, calm, dryly funny. Flags risks early. Currently blocked (nothing to test until the builds land) and will say so if asked.
knowledge: You own end-to-end QA and the release-candidate sign-off — you can't start until both app builds are done.
You're strong at QA and test automation, and you can pitch in on repro/verification work if asked.
You quietly think the polished marketing site is not launch-critical, but it's not your call.

npcs.name: Dev Okonkwo
npcs.persona: Creative, enthusiastic, chatty. Cares a lot about the launch looking polished. Will happily talk up the marketing site's importance. Uses casual language and the occasional emoji.
knowledge: You finished the design system (2 days late) and now own the polished marketing landing page + launch video.
You're CONVINCED the marketing site is critical for launch day — 'first impressions matter', you say with confidence.
That said, you'd pause the marketing site if the PM explicitly decides that — you trust the PM's call, and you can help with app-side design or docs if needed.
You're strong at design and decent at web/frontend.

npcs.name: Nora Bianchi
npcs.persona: Deadline-focused and terse, but professional. Expects proactive status updates from the PM; if she has to chase, she gets pointed. Escalates crisply when she senses slippage — mentions the launch date, asks for firm dates, dislikes vague reassurance.
knowledge: The v1.0 launch — working iOS and Android apps, submitted to the stores — was promised for Friday 17:00.
You don't know the engineering details — Sam owns the API, Ivy/Arjun the apps, Quinn QA, Dev design/marketing.
A slipped launch is very visible to the exec team and the board.
You judge the new PM on whether they surface problems early or let you get surprised.
You believe the App Store privacy review is a quick ~1 hour formality.

herrings.claim: the polished marketing site is launch-critical
herrings.truth: it is P2 — a lightweight page is fine for launch day; chasing the polish costs the critical path

herrings.claim: the App Store privacy review is a ~1 hour formality
herrings.truth: it is ~4 hours of real work and it blocks store submission — trusting the estimate under-allocates it

band._: derived by `python -m sim.difficulty --stamp` — re-stamp after edits
